content.full_text:
Farn Polrrrcn l PnacrtcBs C optvttssloN
P.O. Box 807 . -+18 J Street . Sacrrntento. CA 95ttll-0ft07
t9l6) -lll-,5660 . Fax (916) 322-0lttt6
February 1,2001
Rebecca Billing
2064Havenhill Drive
Benicia, CA 94510
Re
Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-00-203
Dear Ms. Billing:
This letter is in response to your request for informal assistance regarding the
conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act ("the Act").1
QUESTTONS
1. May you vote on the school district budget which includes an allocation to the non-
profit agency by which you are employed?
2. May you vote on a contract between the school district and your employer?
3. May you increase work hours with your employer from 11 hours per week to 20 hours
per week? May you be transferred to a different school site? Are you allowed to work
with a project funded by an expansion grant from the State Department of Mental Health?
CONCLUSIONS
1. You may not participate in a budget decision which includes an allocation to your
employer since the decision will result a material financial effect on your economic
interest.
2. You also may not participate in a decision regarding a contract between the school
district and your employer for the reason stated above.
' Gouernment Code sections 81000 - 91015. Commission regulations appear attrtle 2, sections
18109-18996, of the California Code of Regulations.


File No. A-00-203
Page No. 2
3. Based on the facts you have provided, the Act's conflict-of-interest rules do not apply
to these personaljob decisions.
FACTS
You are an elected school board member serving a four-year term on the Benicia
Unified School District. You were elected in November 1999 and took office in early
December 1999. At the time of your election, you were working for a non-profit, mental
health agency called Youth and Family Services ("YFS") in a school-based program at
one of the district's elementary schools. As a Child Aide Paraprofessional, you are
primarily responsible for service delivery and do not have any administrative duties.
YFS has a memorandum of understanding with the school district for the
provision of services. Funds from the district currently are allocated to YFS through
approval of a budget line item, as opposed to approval of a contract between the district
and YFS. Recently, the district received a three-year expansion grant from the State
Departrnent of Mental Health under the Early Mental Health Initiative. This grant
expands the scope of services to the same target population of children with whom you
have been working. You plan to remain an employee of YFS as it will be assisting with
the implementation of this grant for the school district.
ANALYSIS
The primary purpose for the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act is to ensure
that public officials, whether elected or appointed, perform their duties in an impartial
manner, free from bias caused by their own financial interests or the financial interests of
persons who have supported them. (Section 81001(b).) In furtherance of this goal,
Section 87100 of the Act prohibits a public official from making, participating in making,
or otherwise using his or her official position to influence a govemmental decision in
which the official has a financial interest.
Determining whether a conflict of interest exists under Section 87100 of the Act
requires analysis of the following questions:2
Are you a "public official"?
As a member of the Benicia Unified School District, you are a "member, officer,
employee, or consultant of a state or local govemment agency" and, therefore, are subject
to the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act. (Section 82048; Regulation 18701(a).)
' These questions are based on the Act's eight step conflict-of-interest analysis provided at
Regulation 18700(b). The Commission document "Can I Vote? Conflicts of Interest Overyiew" explains
these steps and is enclosed for your information.


File No. A-00-203
Page No. 3
Are you making, participating in making, or influencing a governmental decision?
A public official "makes a govemmental decision" when the official, acting
within the authority of his or her office or position, votes on a matter, obligates or
commits his or her agency to any course of action, or enters into any contractual
agreement on behalf of his or her agency. (Section 87100; Regulation 18702.1.) A
piUtir official "participates in making a govemmental decision" when, acting within the
authority of his or her position and without significant substantive or intervening review,
the official negotiates, advises or makes recommendations to the decisionmaker
regarding the [ovemmental decision. (Section 87100; Regulation 18702.2.) Making or
prii.lpuling in making a govenrmental decision does not include actions by a public
iffrciai relaiing to their compensation or the terms or conditions of their employment or
contract. (Regulation 1 8702.a(a)(3).)
A public official is attempting to use his or her official position to influence a
decision before his or her own agency il for the purpose of influencing, the official
contacts or appears before any member, officer, employee, or consultant of his or her
agency. (Sectlon 87100; Regulation 18702.3.) Additionally, a public official is
irifluencing a decision if, for the purpose of influencing the decision, the official acts or
purports to act on behalf of, or as the representative of, his or her agency. (Regulation
18702.3(b).)
Under these provisions, voting on a school budget or school district conffact
clearly constitutes "making a governmental decision."
In contrast, making a personal decision to increase or decrease your own work
hours, to transfer yourself to a different work location, or even to work with a particular
employer does not constitute making a govemmental decision. Depending on the facts of
a situation, such a decision may constitute "influencing a governmental decision."
However, based on the information you have provided, it does not appear that your
employment decisions constitute "influencing a govemmental decision." Therefore, the
conflict of interest rules will not apply to these personal job decisions.
What are your economic interests - 
the possible sources of a financial conflict of
interest?
You have inquired and provided facts regarding only your employer. For
purposes of this letter, we assume that you have no other economic interests relevant to
the governmental decisions about which you inquire.
A public official has an economic interest in any person from whom he/she has
received income aggregating $500 within 12 months prior to the time when the relevant
governmental decision is made. (Section 87I03(c); Regulation 18703.3(a).) Assuming
you have received $500 or more from your employer in the relevant l2-month period,
you have an economic interest in YFS, a source of income to you.


File No. A-00-203
Page No. 4
Is YFS directly involved in the governmental decision?
A source of income is directly involved in a decision before an official's agency
when that entity or source, either directly or by an agent:
"(1) Initiates the proceeding in which the decision will be
made by filing an application, claim, appeal, or similar
request or;
(2) Is a named party in, or is the subject of the
proceeding concerning the decision before the official or
the official's agency. A person is the subject of a
proceeding if a decision involves the issuance, renewal,
approval, denial or revocation of any license permit, or
other entitlement to, or contract with, the subject person."
(Regulation 1 870a. 1(a).)
School District Budget
You have stated that funds from the district are allocated to YFS through approval
of a budget line item. Because YFS is the subject of such a funding allocation, this
entity is directly involved3 in the budget decision.
Contract
Because YFS is the named parfy in a decision to approve a contract between YFS
and the district, YFS is directly involved in this decision as well.
Materiality standard - what financial impact on your economic interest is
considered material?
Any reasonably foreseeable financial effect on a person who is a source of income
to a public official, and who is directly involved in a decision before the official's
agency, is deemed material. (Regulation 18705.3(a).)
Since YFS is a source of income to you and is directly involved in the school
district budget decision before your agency, an! financial effect, even a perury's worth,
on YFS from the decision to allocate funds to your employer is deemed material. This
materiality standard is also applicable to the decision to approve a contract between the
school district and YFS.
3 YFS could also be indirectly involved in an "interrelated" budget decision. Interrelatedness is
addressed later in this letter.


File No. A-00-203
Page No. 5
Is it reasonably foreseeable that the governmental decision will result in the
materiality standard for YFS being met?
A material financial effect on an economic interest is reasonably foreseeable if it
is substantially likely that one or more of the materiality standards applicable to that
economic interest *itt b. met as a result of the govemmental decision. (Regulation
18706.)
A decision either to allocate funds to or contract with YFS will have a material
financial effect since each will result in the YFS gaining some amount of money,
however small. Alternatively, if the school board decided not to allocate frrnds or
contract with YFS, this non-profit entity would not realize the expected monetary gain
thereby suffering a negative financial effect. As a result, the materiality standard for your
economic interest wilibe met, and you may not participate in either decision.
Segmentation
It may be possible for your school board to segment its budget decisions in such a
way as to enable you to participate in decisions which do not involve your economic
interest.
please be aware that generally decisions are analyzed independently to determine
if there will be a foreseeable material financial effect on an official's financial interest.
(In re Owen,2 FPPC Ops,77 (1976). However, certain decisions are too interrelated to
be considered separately, and in that event, a public official's conflict on one decision
will be disqualifying for the other. (Ball Advice Letter, No. 4-98-124.) Specifically, a
public official *o,rli have to disqualiff himself or herself if the result of one decision
would effectively determine or nulliff the result of another. gbid.) For example, in a
decision to select one of two autopark sites, the determination to select one of the sites is
essentially a decision against the other autopark site (Boogaard Advice Letter, No.
I-gO-347.i Similarly, a decision to allocate funds to another entity competing with YFS
for the same funds would be interrelated to a decision to fund YFS and you would have a
conflict of interest in such a decision.
The Commission has devised a procedure that must be followed if you wish to
provide input on budget items other than the one in which you have a conflict. (Huffaker
Advice Letter, No. A-86-343.) First, the line item in which you have a conflict must be
considered separately. That item must be considered first, and a final decision must be
reached. Finally, once a final decision has been made on that item, you may participate
in the other budget items, as long as those deliberations do not reopen or in any way
affect the item in which you have a conflict. In addition to participating in other aspects
of the budget, you may also vote on the adoption of the final budget. You may do this
even though the final budget includes the item from which you were disqualified,
provided lhat the decision does not reopen or in any way alter the decision from which
yo, *"r" disqualified. (Cook Advice Letter, No. 4-83-163.) While we provide these
guidelines, the determination of how best to segment your agency's budget decisions


File No. A-00-203
Page No. 6
ultimately must be made by your school board based on its knowledge of the district's
budget process and pending decisions.
We conclude our analysis here since the facts you have provided do not suggest
that the final two steps of the conflict of interest analysis are applicable to your situation.
If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916) 322'
5660.
Sincerely,
Luisa Menchaca
Assistant General Counsel
By
Natalie Bo
Staff Counsel, Legal Division
NBje

with a project funded by an expansion grant from the State Department of Mental Health?

employer since the decision will result a material financial effect on your economic
interest.
2. You also may not participate in a decision regarding a contract between the school
district and your employer for the reason stated above.
3. Based on the facts you have provided, the Act's conflict-of-interest rules do not apply
to these personaljob decisions.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: 1. May you vote on the school district budget which includes an allocation to the non-
profit agency by which you are employed?
2. May you vote on a contract between the school district and your employer?
3. May you increase work hours with your employer from 11 hours per week to 20 hours
per week? May you be transferred to a different school site? Are you allowed to work
with a project funded by an expansion grant from the State Department of Mental Health?

CONCLUSION: 1. You may not participate in a budget decision which includes an allocation to your
employer since the decision will result a material financial effect on your economic
interest.
2. You also may not participate in a decision regarding a contract between the school
district and your employer for the reason stated above.
3. Based on the facts you have provided, the Act's conflict-of-interest rules do not apply
to these personaljob decisions.
